relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Paul asks them to bear with a little foolishness.
His jealousy is protective, wanting them wholly for Messiah.
He fears craftiness corrupting their simplicity toward Messiah.
They tolerate alternate Jesus, spirit, and good news too easily.
Paul refuses inferiority to the so-called super-apostles.
He may lack polished speech but not knowledge.
His free proclamation was humility for their exaltation.
Other assemblies supported his service to Corinth.
He refused to burden them even in lack.
This boast remains open in Achaia.
His refusal to take from them is not lack of love.
He cuts off opportunity from status-boasters.
False apostles are deceitful workers wearing Messiah-language.
Even adversarial appearance can mimic light.
Servants of deception can mimic justice-language.
Paul accepts the role of fool to expose false boasting.
This boasting is named as foolishness, not the Lord’s normal way.
Since others boast by flesh, Paul enters the comparison ironically.
Their claimed wisdom has tolerated fools.
They have tolerated people who enslave, devour, exploit, exalt themselves, and strike.
Paul names his apparent weakness and then dares to compare.
He shares Hebrew, Israelite, Abrahamic identity.
His service is marked by labor, prison, blows, and frequent danger of death.
He bears repeated lashings from his own people.
Rods, stones, shipwrecks, and the deep mark his path.
Every road brings danger: river, robber, city, wilderness, sea, false siblings.
Toil, sleeplessness, hunger, thirst, fasting, cold, and nakedness become the ledger.
Beyond external dangers, daily concern for the assemblies presses him.
Their weakness becomes his weakness; their stumbling burns in him.
If boasting must happen, he boasts in weakness.
The blessed Divine knows he is not lying.
Damascus was guarded to seize him.
He escapes lowered through a wall in a basket.

contemplative_note: 2 Corinthians 11 unmasks false light by weakness. The true servant does not enslave or devour; the true servant carries wounds, concern, and costly love.
